title_en: Taharah and Salah: The Hanafi Basics
content_id:
Mukhtasar dibuka dengan taharah - kesucian ritual - dan penempatannya mencerminkan prinsip yang mengalir melalui semua manual fikih klasik: keabsahan ibadah bergantung pada syarat-syarat yang mendahuluinya. Al-Quduri menyajikan hukum-hukum Hanafi tentang bersuci dengan ekonomi yang khas, mencakup setiap kategori air, setiap jenis najis, dan setiap metode bersuci dalam pernyataan-pernyataan singkat yang tepat kata-katanya sehingga tidak ada ruang untuk ambiguitas.
Dalam pandangan Hanafi, air yang digunakan untuk bersuci dibagi menjadi tiga kategori: air suci dan menyucikan, air suci tetapi tidak menyucikan, dan air najis. Kategori pertama - tahir mutahhir - adalah air dalam keadaan aslinya, tidak tersentuh oleh najis apa pun. Kategori kedua muncul ketika air telah digunakan dalam suatu tindakan bersuci sebelumnya, seperti air yang menetes dari anggota tubuh saat wudu. Al-Quduri mencatat bahwa posisi Hanafi mayoritas menyatakan bahwa air bekas ini suci tetapi tidak lagi mampu menyampaikan kesucian ritual - posisi yang membedakan mazhab Hanafi dari Syafi'i, yang mengizinkan penggunaannya kembali. Kategori ketiga mencakup air yang telah bercampur dengan najis hingga berubah warna, rasa, atau baunya.
Bagian tentang wudu (ablusi ritual) mencakup empat tindakan wajib: membasuh wajah, membasuh lengan hingga siku, mengusap seperempat kepala, dan membasuh kaki hingga mata kaki. Al-Quduri menyatakan posisi-posisi Hanafi tentang minimum yang diperlukan tanpa elaborasi panjang. Ia mencatat syarat-syarat yang membatalkan wudu - tidur, hilang kesadaran, keluarnya darah atau nanah, dan sejenisnya - lagi-lagi dalam gaya deklaratif singkat yang mendefinisikan Mukhtasar.
Mengenai ghusl (mandi ritual penuh), al-Quduri mencakup keadaan-keadaan yang mewajibkannya: janabah (ketidaksucian ritual besar setelah hubungan intim atau ejakulasi), akhir menstruasi, dan akhir pendarahan nifas. Tiga unsur wajib ghusl dalam mazhab Hanafi adalah berkumur, membersihkan hidung, dan membasuh seluruh tubuh. Ini adalah konsepsi yang lebih luas tentang tindakan wajib ghusl dibandingkan beberapa mazhab lain, di mana berkumur dan membersihkan hidung dianggap sebagai Sunnah dan bukan fardhu.
Bagian tentang shalat dalam Mukhtasar mencakup syarat-syarat keabsahannya, unsur-unsur wajibnya (fardhu), unsur-unsur yang diperlukan (wajib), dan Sunnah-Sunnah yang dikukuhkan. Perbedaan Hanafi antara fardhu dan wajib adalah salah satu tanda metodologis mazhab: tindakan wajib memang wajib tetapi jika kelalaiannya tidak disengaja dapat dikompensasi dengan sujud kelupaan (sajdat al-sahw), sedangkan meninggalkan unsur fardhu akan membatalkan shalat sepenuhnya. Al-Quduri menyajikan daftar-daftar yang relevan tanpa argumentasi, mempercayakan kepada pelajar untuk menginternalisasi kategori-kategori sebelum mencari penalaran di baliknya.
Membaca bab-bab taharah dan shalat Mukhtasar bersama sebuah komentar mengungkapkan betapa banyak pemikiran hukum yang cermat dimampatkan ke dalam kalimat-kalimat singkat al-Quduri. Setiap pernyataan memiliki sejarah diskusi keilmuan di baliknya, dan nilai Mukhtasar justru karena ia menyaring sejarah tersebut ke dalam bentuk yang dapat dibawa pelajar ke depan.
content_en:
The Mukhtasar opens with taharah — ritual purity — and the placement reflects a principle that runs through all classical fiqh manuals: the validity of worship depends on the conditions that precede it. Al-Quduri presents the Hanafi rulings on purification with characteristic economy, covering each category of water, each type of impurity, and each method of purification in brief, precisely worded statements that leave no room for ambiguity.

In the Hanafi view, water used for purification is divided into three categories: pure and purifying water, pure but non-purifying water, and impure water. The first category — tahir mutahhir — is water in its original state, untouched by any impurity. The second category arises when water has been used in a previous act of purification, such as water that has dripped from the limbs during wudu. Al-Quduri notes that the majority Hanafi position holds this used water to be pure but no longer capable of conveying ritual purification — a position that distinguishes the Hanafi school from the Shafi'is, who permit its reuse. The third category covers water that has been mixed with impurity (najasah) to the degree that it has changed in color, taste, or smell.

The section on wudu (ritual ablution) covers the four obligatory acts: washing the face, washing the arms to the elbows, wiping a quarter of the head, and washing the feet to the ankles. Al-Quduri states the Hanafi positions on the minimum required without extensive elaboration. He notes the conditions that nullify wudu — sleep, loss of consciousness, emission of blood or pus, and the like — again in the terse, declarative style that defines the Mukhtasar.

On ghusl (full ritual bath), al-Quduri covers the circumstances that make it obligatory: janabah (major ritual impurity following intercourse or ejaculation), the end of menstruation, and the end of postpartum bleeding. The three obligatory elements of ghusl in the Hanafi school are rinsing the mouth, rinsing the nose, and washing the entire body. This is a broader conception of the ghusl's obligatory acts than in some other schools, where rinsing the mouth and nose are considered sunnah rather than fard.

The section on salah in the Mukhtasar covers the conditions for its validity, its obligatory elements (fard), its necessary elements (wajib), and its confirmed sunnahs. The Hanafi distinction between fard and wajib is one of the school's methodological signatures: a wajib act is obligatory but its omission, if unintentional, can be compensated for with a prostration of forgetfulness (sajdat al-sahw), whereas omitting a fard element invalidates the prayer entirely. Al-Quduri presents the relevant lists without argument, trusting the student to internalize the categories before seeking the reasoning behind them.

Reading the taharah and salah chapters of the Mukhtasar alongside a commentary reveals how much careful legal thinking is compressed into al-Quduri's terse sentences. Each statement has a history of scholarly discussion behind it, and the Mukhtasar's value is precisely that it distills that history into a form the student can carry forward.
content_ar: The Mukhtasar opens with الطهارة — ritual purity — and the placement reflects a principle that runs through all الكلاسيكي الفقه manuals: the validity of worship depends on the conditions that precede it. Al-Quduri presents the الحنفي rulings on purification with characteristic economy, covering each category of water, each type of impurity, and each method of purification in brief, precisely worded statements that leave no room for ambiguity. In the الحنفي view, water used for purification is divided into three categories: pure and purifying water, pure but non-purifying water, and impure water. The أولاً category — tahir mutahhir — is water in its original state, untouched by any impurity. The ثانياً category arises عندما water has been used in a previous act of purification, مثل water that has dripped from the limbs خلال الوضوء. Al-Quduri notes that the majority الحنفي position holds this used water to be pure but no longer capable of conveying ritual purification — a position that distinguishes the الحنفي school from the Shafi'is, who permit its reuse. The ثالثاً category covers water that has been mixed with impurity (النجاسة) to the degree that it has changed in color, taste, or smell. The الفصل on الوضوء (ritual ablution) covers the four obligatory acts: washing the face, washing the arms to the elbows, wiping a quarter of the head, and washing the feet to the ankles. Al-Quduri states the الحنفي positions on the minimum required without extensive elaboration. He notes the conditions that nullify الوضوء — sleep, loss of consciousness, emission of blood or pus, and the like — again in the terse, declarative style that defines the Mukhtasar. On الغسل (full ritual bath), al-Quduri covers the circumstances that make it obligatory: janabah (major ritual impurity following intercourse or ejaculation), the end of menstruation, and the end of postpartum bleeding. The three obligatory elements of الغسل in the الحنفي school are rinsing the mouth, rinsing the nose, and washing the entire الجسد. This is a broader conception of the الغسل's obligatory acts than in some other schools, where rinsing the mouth and nose are considered السنة rather than الفرض. The الفصل on الصلاة in the Mukhtasar covers the conditions for its validity, its obligatory elements (الفرض), its necessary elements (الواجب), and its confirmed sunnahs. The الحنفي distinction between الفرض and الواجب is one of the school's methodological signatures: a الواجب act is obligatory but its omission, إذا unintentional, can be compensated for with a prostration of forgetfulness (sajdat al-sahw), في حين omitting a الفرض element invalidates the الصلاة entirely. Al-Quduri presents the relevant lists without argument, trusting the student to internalize the categories قبل seeking the reasoning behind them. Reading the الطهارة and الصلاة الأبواب of the Mukhtasar alongside a commentary reveals how much careful legal thinking is compressed into al-Quduri's terse sente
